prompt: I launched a Google Ads campaign on July 1st, 2024 that ran through September 30th. I have daily sales data from January through September, along with organic traffic and competitor sales as control metrics. The campaign cost $50,000 total. Help me measure the incremental impact using CausalImpact. The average order value is $120.
expectations: Uses CausalImpact library correctly
Defines pre_period as January-June and post_period as July-September
Includes appropriate control variables (organic traffic, competitor sales)
Calculates incremental sales with confidence intervals
Computes incremental ROAS using the $50,000 spend and $120 AOV
Provides statistical significance (p-value)
Creates visualization showing actual vs predicted
Interprets results in business context

prompt: I need to compare the incrementality of three marketing channels: Google Ads ($50k spend), Meta Ads ($75k spend), and TikTok Ads ($25k). Each channel has its own campaign period and I want to know which delivers the best incremental ROAS to optimize my budget allocation. I have separate datasets for each channel with appropriate controls.
expectations: Runs CausalImpact analysis separately for each channel
Calculates incremental ROAS for each channel
Creates a comparison visualization or table across channels
Accounts for different spend levels when comparing efficiency
Provides clear budget allocation recommendation based on results
Reports statistical significance for each channel
Explains which channel has highest incremental efficiency (revenue per $1k spent)

prompt: Before trusting my incrementality results, I want to validate that my CausalImpact model assumptions hold. I have a campaign that ran in August 2024. Help me check: (1) whether my control variables were affected by the intervention, (2) if the model fits well in the pre-period, and (3) run a placebo test on the pre-period to check for false positives.
expectations: Plots control variables across pre/post periods to check for contamination
Evaluates pre-period model fit (mentions R-squared or similar metric)
Runs a placebo test using only pre-period data with fake intervention date
Interprets placebo test p-value (should be > 0.05)
Checks residual autocorrelation or provides diagnostic plots
Provides clear validation summary stating whether assumptions hold
Recommends whether to trust the main analysis results

prompt: I want to run a geographic holdout test. I'll exclude paid marketing from two test cities (Austin and Portland) while keeping it running in two control cities (Seattle and Denver). Help me design and analyze this experiment to measure the incremental impact of the marketing campaigns. The test runs for 8 weeks starting March 1st, 2024.
expectations: Explains that test regions should show NEGATIVE effect (miss out on lift)
Uses control region sales as synthetic control for test regions
Defines proper pre-period (before March 1st) and post-period (March 1 - April 25)
Configures CausalImpact correctly for geographic holdout design
Interprets the negative effect as the incremental value of marketing
Discusses whether the effect is statistically significant
Provides estimate of what would happen if marketing was paused nationwide

prompt: I have multiple potential control variables (organic traffic, competitor sales, seasonality index, weather index) and I'm not sure which combination to use. Run a sensitivity analysis to test how robust my incrementality estimate is across different control variable specifications.
expectations: Tests multiple combinations of control variables
Runs CausalImpact with each control set
Compares effect estimates across specifications
Reports range of incremental effect estimates
Assesses whether results are robust or sensitive to control choice
Recommends a preferred specification with justification
Creates table or visualization comparing sensitivity results

prompt: My Google Ads dashboard shows $500,000 in attributed revenue (last-click attribution), but I suspect this overstates the true incremental impact. I ran a holdout test and have the data to measure true incrementality. Help me compare the platform attribution to the causal impact and calculate the attribution inflation factor.
expectations: Runs CausalImpact to measure true incremental revenue
Compares incremental revenue to the $500k platform attribution
Calculates attribution inflation factor (platform / incremental)
Explains the difference between correlation and causation
Provides incremental ROAS using true incremental revenue
Discusses implications for marketing measurement and optimization
Visualizes the comparison between attributed and incremental revenue

prompt: I want to understand how the campaign impact evolved over time. Did it have immediate effects or delayed effects? Did it diminish over time due to saturation? Split the 12-week campaign period into 3 four-week windows and analyze how the daily incremental effect changed across these periods.
expectations: Splits post-period into multiple time windows
Runs separate CausalImpact analysis for each window
Calculates daily incremental effect for each period
Compares effect magnitude across windows
Identifies whether effects are immediate, delayed, or show saturation
Creates visualization showing effect evolution over time
Provides interpretation about campaign dynamics and implications

prompt: I have 2 weeks of pre-campaign data and want to measure the impact of a 4-week campaign. Is this enough data? What problems might I encounter and what should I do about it?
expectations: Identifies that 2 weeks pre-period is insufficient for 4 week post-period
Explains the rule of thumb (3x post-period length for pre-period)
Discusses problems: poor model fit, high uncertainty, unreliable estimates
Recommends either extending pre-period or shortening post-period
Suggests alternative approaches if data constraints cannot be resolved
Mentions that results will have very wide confidence intervals
Does NOT proceed with analysis given the insufficient data

prompt: I'm running a Google Ads campaign where I started with €200/day on July 1st, then increased to €400/day on July 15th, €600/day on August 1st, and €800/day on August 15th. I want to measure the incremental impact and understand if I'm seeing diminishing returns. I have daily sales data from January through September, with organic traffic and competitor sales as controls.
expectations: FIRST asks about intervention type and recognizes this as progressive/gradual change
Presents the 3-4 analysis options (Dose-Response, Phase-by-Phase, Covariate, Discrete)
Recommends Option 1 (Dose-Response) or Option 3 (Phase-by-Phase) with clear reasoning
Explains advantages of recommended approach for this specific case
Waits for user confirmation before proceeding with analysis
If using dose-response: includes daily_spend as a predictor variable
If using phase-by-phase: analyzes marginal effect of each budget increase
Discusses diminishing returns based on results
Calculates incremental ROAS that accounts for varying spend levels
Provides recommendation on optimal budget level based on marginal returns

prompt: We launched our campaign in Madrid on July 1st with €1000/day spend. Then on July 8th we added Barcelona with an additional €800/day. On July 15th we added Valencia with €600/day. And on July 22nd we went nationwide with €2000/day total. I want to see which expansion generated the most incremental value per euro spent.
expectations: Identifies this as a phased geographic rollout (stepped intervention)
Presents analysis options and recommends Option 3 (Phase-by-Phase)
Explains that each phase will show the marginal impact of adding that geography
Confirms approach with user before proceeding
Defines 4 clear phases with appropriate pre/post periods
For each phase, uses cumulative prior phases as baseline
Calculates incremental sales per euro for each geographic expansion
Identifies which geography had highest efficiency (sales per €)
Shows diminishing returns if present across phases
Provides visualization or table comparing phase performance
